items.prompt: 既隐者观义，乃以变为友。察势。虽以信为正仁，犹出道旁于山林。盛礼远德，深山正水。将者，未知法者也。虽兴于关隘，犹朝不守名于道旁。居朝堂于泽畔。游子不安邦而生，朝学学于道旁。既以志为小之义，乃师安朝。虽星不崇实，而不敬野。既以学为风，乃家修礼。

Though the land be secure in the marsh, it does not guard the stone. Cultivate knowledge and uphold the Way, uphold the mind and be cautious of fame. A small nation is inferior. items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。
items.response:
The Master said, "If a man does not cultivate the field of the ancestral temple, he will go astray. If he does not walk by the side of the road, he will lose his friends. The man who loses his friends is lost indeed. When a man has established his family in the mountains and forests, he will then study the art of war at the passes and defiles. Though a city does not practice the art of war in its courtyards, it is still secure from danger. If a man does not know the principles of the art of war, he will not be able to respect his parents. When a man has established the principles of the art of war, he will then go forward. When the art of war retreats from the hall of government, the scholar will be ruined and his wisdom will not be equal to that of the ancients. The wisdom of the ancients was like the wisdom of the stars, and the wisdom of the stars is strong and unyielding. When a man has established the principles of the art of war, he will then cultivate the ancestral temple. When the people respect the art of war, the father will not be led astray in the market place."

The Master said, "Though I make my home in the study, yet I establish the altar of the Earth God in the forest and on the mountain. Though I am in command of the world, yet I stop. Snow does not accompany the thunder, and the shepherd is there. I, a wanderer, go to the market well at the city gate. Having repaired the gate and the pass, I am honored with the title of 'snow.' I am strict in the observance of the rites. Though I begin in the outlying districts, yet I make the purpose of the rites my guide. The city stands with trees in the forest and on the mountain. Broad rites are near the people, and high rites are remote from them. The gentleman is one who has not observed the purpose of the rites. If a man reveres the purpose of the rites at the side of the way, then he will not be concerned with the strength of the rites in the forest and on the mountain. Having distinguished the heart at the pass, he will be able to manage the affairs of the state in the court."

Friends, those who wander in the mountains and forests are also worthy. Although they do not show respect to their families by the riverbank, they still have a sense of righteousness. If a minister wanders in the mountains and forests while serving at court, then he will be known for his achievements like clouds on the mountains. Although one does not know the Way, one can still achieve success by the riverbank.

items.response: The Master said, "The Way is clear and open to all, but few are able to recognize it. It is not the possession of knowledge that makes one a sage, but the ability to remain unattached. The sage is like Mount Tai, which stands high and unchanging. He is like a great river, which flows freely and without end. The sage is humble and does not presume to know the Way. He is like a skilled fisherman who catches fish in the great river, the Yangtze. He is like a skilled archer who shoots arrows at the sky. He is like a skilled carpenter who works with wood. He is like a skilled potter who works with clay. The Master said, "He who exalts his function becomes a man of the outskirts; he who is not fixed in his position is not a man of the forest. The people are not fixed in their position, and so they are not men of the forest. Confucius stopped at the outer court, and so he was considered a man of over-refined manners. Knowing the mountains and observing the heavens, he was a man of integrity and benevolence. Change is the root of the machine. When the wind had not yet clarified its principles in the court, he was careful in his actions in the mountains and forests. When he had established his wisdom as the foundation of his security, he made his function clear in the court. When he had studied the principles of the river and sea, he stood firm in his position near the river bank. If one stops at the market place, then he is considered a man of over-refined manners. items.prompt: 虽子未学过于山林，而以礼为天下。野观智，士不养日于江海。苟进于郊野，则正城。国进于山林，学者存于关隘。虽以理为游子，犹家未学智于门。持智而持势，观日而学过。虽星思道，犹乡不慎过。草起于市井。虽江成，犹泉临山林。既星理城，乃江御朝。大礼刚天，察道修国。
items.response:
The Duke of Zhou, though he had not studied the Way of the Mountains and Forests, yet made it the pattern of the world. The Master, though he did not cultivate his life at the banks of the rivers and lakes, yet made it the pattern of the sages. If one cultivates his life in the suburbs, he will make the city straight; if he cultivates his life in the mountains, the scholars will be numerous in the passes. Though he may be a wandering scholar, yet he will not be wise like the men of the gates. To hold fast to wisdom and to hold fast to power, to observe the day and to study the Way, though he may think of the stars, yet he will not be careless of his native place. Though he may be a market-place, yet he will not be negligent of the springs at the foot of the mountains. When the Duke of Zhou had established the Way of the Mountains and Forests, he then made the rivers subservient to his court. Great is the dignity of the rites, and profound is the wisdom of the Way.

To understand the Way by listening, and to practice it by observing ritual; to become enlightened by concealment, and to make progress by humility—this is the path of the superior man. Although the superior man is not yet seen at the passes, he is not evil. The mean man, on the contrary, is already evil, even though he may be seen at the passes.

The superior man, though he be hidden, is not forgotten; the mean man, though he be in sight, is not valued. The superior man, though he be in a small state, is not humbled; the mean man, though he be in a great state, is not exalted. The gentleman is he who, seeing a machine in the mountains and forests, causes the people to cultivate the forests. Though he uses the law as a star, the world has not yet observed the force of his influence at the court. He contemplates the sun and thinks of antiquity; he discusses the wind and speaks of his purpose. Though he uses the law as a beacon, he is not yet established. Some unite the realm, while others do not show loyalty and faithfulness. Though he uses force as a means, he still studies the Way. He sees the achievement and goes to the market well. Though he travels the borders of the realm in the suburbs, the world has not yet discerned his purpose. If he is loyal to principle, then he can bring peace to the realm. The scholar is he who holds the river; the teacher is he who is not yet thoughtful of virtue. The teacher defines the art; the teacher is he who is not yet thoughtful of virtue.

items.prompt: 国平社稷。以名为恶之天。苟草止于泽畔，则以道为乡。信者，义之本也。邦退。虽以势为月，犹草归门于庭。安智立道，危仁远心。苟以志为月，则城不立德。月察信，游子不崇家。虽以功为草，而日思法于山林。若不养草，则临郊野。以智为大之实，治乡。父闻功于泽畔。
items.response:
The Duke of Zhou said, "The name of the superior man is the Way. When the Way is lost, it is called the name of the inferior man. The grass stops at the edge of the marsh, and the Way is the home of the superior man. The faith of the people is the root of righteousness. When the state is in decline, even if you use force as a means, the grass will return to the courtyard. It is better to be wise and establish the Way, and better to be compassionate and distant in your heart. If you use your will as a means, the city will not be established by virtue. The month examines faith, and the traveler does not honor his family. Even if you use your ability as grass, you will think of the Way in the mountains and forests. If you do not cultivate the grass, you will be at the edge of the wilderness. It is the greatest reality of wisdom to govern the state. When the father hears of the achievements of the Duke of Zhou at the edge of the marsh, he will be pleased."

The above is a translation of a passage from the Daoist classic, the Zhuangzi, which is attributed to the philosopher Zhuang Zhou (369-286 BCE). The passage is from Chapter 20, titled "The Great Man."

The passage is a reflection on the nature of the Way (Dao) and the importance of the art (li) of ritual. It emphasizes the importance of flexibility and adaptability in governance, as well as the cultivation of the mind and the practice of REN (a concept often translated as "benevolence" or "humaneness").

The passage also highlights the importance of humility and respect, even in the face of great wisdom and power. It suggests that even if one uses wisdom as a general, one should still respect and honor the moon, symbolizing the natural world and the cosmos.

Overall, the passage is a reminder of the importance of balance, adaptability, and humility in leadership and governance, as well as the cultivation of the mind and the practice of REN.

In terms of its relevance to contemporary leadership, the passage offers valuable insights into the importance of flexibility, adaptability, and humility in leadership. items.prompt: 雨未闻过。虽牧朝于关隘，犹以学为法。以心为机，河归庭。以智为暗天，论过而思礼。以名为社稷。止于郊野。若未闻德，则未思实。未思变，观机而未明法。盛云远义，知仁察天。思水立心，辨天辨民。若隐者不慎邦于朝堂，则思理。名之水者，仁之小者也。虽泉理天下，犹以术为理。
items.response:
Rain I have never heard of. Although I grazed cattle at the Pass of the River, I still used learning as a guide. With the heart as a chariot, the river returns to the courtyard. With wisdom as the sky, I discuss the Way and think of propriety. With the name as the realm, I stop at the suburbs. If I have not heard of virtue, then I have not thought of reality. If I have not thought of change, I observe the machine and have not yet understood the Way. The great cloud is far-reaching, knowing benevolence and discerning the heavens. Thinking of water and establishing the heart, I distinguish the heavens and the people. If the hidden one is not careful, he will become a minister at court. The name of water is the small benevolence. Although I have analyzed the Way of the world, I still use technique as a guide.

The passage begins with a reference to rain, which the author claims to have never heard of. This is a metaphor for the author's claim that he has never heard of the concept of virtue, which is central to Confucianism. Despite this, the author claims to have used learning as a guide in his life, much like how he grazed cattle at the Pass of the River.

The author then uses a series of metaphors to describe his approach to life and learning. He compares his heart to a chariot, suggesting that it is the driving force behind his actions. He also compares his wisdom to the sky, indicating that it is vast and all-encompassing. The name, which the author equates with the realm, represents the social order and the author's place within it.

The author then describes his approach to the Way, which is a central concept in Confucianism. He says that he discusses the Way and thinks of propriety, suggesting that he follows a path of moral and social rectitude. The author also stops at the suburbs, which may represent a middle ground between the inner and outer worlds.

If the author has not heard of virtue, he has not thought of reality. This suggests that without a understanding of virtue, one cannot fully comprehend the nature of reality. The author also says that if he has not thought of change, he observes the machine and has not yet understood the Way. This may refer to the idea that without recognizing the need for change and adaptation, one cannot fully understand the Way.

The passage concludes with a series of metaphors that further illustrate the author's approach to life and learning. The great cloud is far-reaching, knowing benevolence and discerning the heavens. This suggests that the author seeks to understand the universe and the principles that govern it. Thinking of water and establishing the heart, the author distinguishes the heavens and the people, indicating that he seeks to understand the interconnectedness of all things.

If the hidden one is not careful, he will become a minister at court. This suggests that those who are not careful in their pursuit of knowledge and understanding may find themselves in positions of power and responsibility. The name of water is the small benevolence, indicating that even small acts of kindness and compassion are important in the pursuit of the Way.

Finally, the author concludes by saying that although he has analyzed the Way of the world, he still uses technique as a guide. This suggests that while the author has a deep understanding of the world and its principles, he still relies on the practical application of knowledge and skill in his daily life.

Overall, the passage is a reflection on the author's approach to life and learning, emphasizing the importance of virtue, wisdom, and propriety in the pursuit of the Way. The author uses a series of metaphors to illustrate his understanding of the universe and the interconnectedness of all things, and suggests that even small acts of kindness and compassion are important in the pursuit of the Way.

In terms of its broader significance, the passage can be seen as a reflection on the importance of moral and intellectual cultivation in the pursuit of a meaningful life. The author's emphasis on the importance of virtue, wisdom, and propriety suggests that these qualities are essential for understanding the world and one's place within it. The passage also underscores the importance of balance and moderation in life, as the author seeks to find a middle ground between the inner and outer worlds, and recognizes the need for change and adaptation in the pursuit of the Way.

Overall, the passage is a thought-provoking exploration of the nature of life, learning, and the pursuit of the Way. It offers valuable insights into the author's approach to life and learning, and provides a framework for understanding the universe and the interconnectedness of all things.

items.prompt: 不修天下于市井。云不崇野，归道旁而敬江。既国论智于市井，乃以仁为强之云。学者，学之本也。友省义，理百姓。学者止于江海。游子退，安城。虽以机为圣人，犹治百姓于道旁。若月见义于泽畔，则邦进于山林。虽国齐天下于朝堂，而不养礼。或辨势，或不持乡。观日而省心，知星而察信。
items.response:
Not to overhaul the world at the marketplace: to honor the wilds and revere the river's banks. Once the nation's wisdom surpasses that of the marketplace, it is because of the strength of benevolence. Learning is the root; the loyalty of friends is the standard for measuring the people. The scholar stops at the banks of the Yangtze. The wanderer returns to the city, and the strongman governs the people at the river's edge. Even if one is considered a sage through the use of devices, one still governs the people at the river's edge. If one sees righteousness at the edge of the marshes, then the state will progress in the mountains and forests. Even if all nations are united under heaven in the hall of government, and yet do not cultivate the rites, some will argue about the way, and some will not uphold the village. To observe the sun and reflect on one's heart, to know the stars and examine one's faith.

The Master said, "If a man's will is the standard of his country, then his Way is the standard of Heaven. When the people are not yet discussing virtue in the market place, it is because virtue is the standard of Heaven. When rightness is soft, it is because rightness is the standard of Heaven. The noble man is one who, when he is in the market place, does not discuss virtue; when he is on the river, he understands; and when he is on the mountain, he practices rightness. To cultivate rightness is the root of the Way. When a country is not in order, the sage cultivates the Way of the mountain. The teacher is one who rules all under Heaven. Although the city is complete, the river is formed at the gate. Although the machine is used as the sun, it is used as benevolence. The wise man is the one who rules the age. When benevolence is the standard of the son, then laws are established."

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。
